title: Uber’s Autonomous Vehicle Strategy: Slow Their Adoption
gist: Uber, having abandoned building its own self-driving cars, is now lobbying state and city legislatures (New Jersey, Washington DC) to mandate 'hybrid networks' that would require driverless-ride-hailing platforms to route through aggregators like Uber rather than operate standalone apps — a strategy WIRED documents through leaked lobbying language that would specifically restrict Waymo, Zoox, and Tesla robotaxis, even as Uber publicly frames the push as safety-driven caution.

insights.claim: Uber CEO Dara Khosrowshahi told investors in 2024 that Uber wants to be 'the go-to commercial platform' for many AV players rather than build its own self-driving cars, and the company has since signed agreements with more than 25 major robotaxi players, including Waymo, Nuro, Baidu, and Volkswagen's MOIA.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Uber has pivoted from AV developer to AV aggregator/marketplace, meaning its long-term value proposition rests on demand-side network effects (rider app dominance) rather than owning autonomous-driving technology — a key differentiator versus Waymo/Tesla in any AV-exposure investment thesis.
insights.quote: We think there are going to be many AV players around the world, and we want to be the go-to commercial platform for all of them.

insights.claim: Uber lobbyists circulated legislative language in New Jersey that would, for three years, require any platform offering driverless ride-hailing to have human drivers serve 85 percent of its rides — language that would likely bar AV developers like Waymo, Zoox, and Tesla from operating their own standalone ride-hail apps in the state.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: A concrete, documented example of Uber using state-level lobbying to structurally limit competition from AV developers launching their own apps — a material regulatory-risk factor for Tesla's robotaxi ambitions and other standalone AV-app strategies.
insights.locator: In New Jersey, a lobbyist representing Uber

insights.claim: The New Jersey bill under consideration would be the first in the nation to specifically limit Tesla's robotaxis by requiring AV developers to use multiple sensor types rather than cameras alone (Tesla's approach) and to include steering wheels and brake pedals for emergency human operation, which purpose-built robotaxis like Zoox's lack.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: A first-of-its-kind state mandate targeting Tesla's camera-only sensor approach and Zoox's steering-wheel-free design is a specific, name-checked regulatory threat to two AV developers' technology and deployment roadmaps.
insights.locator: The New Jersey bill is the first proposed

insights.claim: In Washington, DC, Uber lobbyist LáVita Gardner secured a commitment from a city council staffer more than a week before a driverless-vehicle bill's introduction that ride-hail companies like Uber would be allowed to participate in the district's autonomous vehicle program, framing 'hybrid networks' as critical for a smooth transition.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Documents Uber pre-shaping legislation before public introduction via direct lobbyist-staffer engagement — evidence of Uber's active, successful influence over emerging AV regulatory frameworks in a major market.
insights.quote: Allowing for hybrid networks will be critical for a smooth transition that supports both technology and human drivers.
insights.locator: In an email sent more than a week before

insights.claim: Uber director of autonomous vehicles and AI policy Harry Hartfield is set to testify to the DC City Council that in California, AV competition has already reduced driver earnings, and that 'one AV in California now performs roughly the work of four drivers.'
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: A striking 4:1 labor-displacement ratio cited by Uber's own policy testimony is a concrete data point for modeling AV-driven labor substitution economics in ride-hail markets and the resulting driver-earnings/labor-union pressure that shapes AV regulation.
insights.quote: One AV in California now performs roughly the work of four drivers.
insights.locator: In prepared testimony set to be delivered

themes.theme: Uber's public rationale vs. lobbying reality

insights.claim: Uber spokesperson Noah Edwardsen says the company supports AV expansion but views industry policy proposals as 'largely unworkable' because they fail to address workers' issues or 'have tried to cynically lock out competitors and create monopolies,' arguing the failure to pass AV regulation in states like Maryland and New York this year justifies Uber's alternative approach.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Uber's public framing positions its own lobbying as a pro-labor compromise, a narrative investors should weigh against the documented New Jersey 85%-human-driver language that would concretely restrict AV-developer competition.
insights.quote: Both the New Jersey and DC proposals that we've opposed have elements that would reduce AV competition.
insights.locator: In a written statement to WIRED

insights.claim: Waymo spokesperson Ethan Teicher states the company 'does not support efforts to limit AVs to specific types of networks' and would welcome legislative clarification allowing different network types to operate — a direct rebuttal to Uber's hybrid-network mandates.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Confirms an active competitive/regulatory conflict between Uber and its own AV partner Waymo, despite their commercial partnership in cities like Atlanta and Austin — signals partnership friction that could affect Waymo's app-distribution strategy and Uber's exclusivity leverage.
insights.locator: In a written statement, Waymo spokesperson

insights.claim: Despite being commercial partners offering exclusive driverless Waymo rides via the Uber app in Atlanta and Austin, Uber and Waymo have shown signs of a strained relationship: Uber's CTO publicly flagged a 'scary Waymo moment' near-miss with a bus in April, and the companies discontinued a more limited pilot partnership in Phoenix last month.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Concrete evidence of partnership strain (public safety callout, discontinued pilot) between the two largest players in the ride-hail/AV aggregation space is a material relationship-risk signal for both companies' platform strategies.
insights.locator: Waymo and Uber are business partners

insights.claim: Waymo leads the US driverless-vehicle race, providing 500,000 rides per week across 11 metro areas, with plans to expand further in 2026 including into London.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: A hard scale metric (500K weekly rides, 11 metros, international expansion to London) benchmarks Waymo's lead in commercial AV deployment, the key comparator against Tesla's robotaxi ambitions and Uber's aggregation model.

insights.claim: Uber's current cautious, regulation-embracing AV posture is a reversal from its history: in 2016 Uber shipped its testing AVs from California to Arizona to avoid seeking a state permit, and in 2018 an Uber self-driving test vehicle struck and killed a pedestrian in Arizona, leading Uber to shut down its AV program entirely in 2020.
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: Uber's own troubled AV-development history (including a fatal 2018 crash) is part of why it exited AV manufacturing and pivoted to the aggregator/lobbying strategy — critical context for assessing the credibility of Uber's current 'safety-first, phased transition' messaging.

insights.claim: Uber president and COO Andrew Macdonald publicly acknowledged in a May LinkedIn post that Uber's new pro-regulation stance 'might feel ironic' given the company's history of insufficient engagement with the societal implications of its earlier growth tactics, which caused 'regulatory battles and a corporate crisis that damaged trust for years.'
insights.speaker: Aarian Marshall
insights.so_what: A rare public admission from Uber leadership of past regulatory misconduct frames the current lobbying strategy as a deliberate reputational pivot — relevant context for how regulators and the public may weigh Uber's current claims of good-faith 'hybrid network' advocacy.
insights.quote: We didn't engage enough with the societal implications or consider how the world outside the walls of Uber HQ felt about how we were going about building.
insights.locator: In a LinkedIn post published in May
